entry.title: PMGM - Display Overall Rating from Previous Performance Forms
entry.content:
Subject: PMGM - Display Overall Rating from Previous Performance Forms (T1/T2/T3)

Description:

We have a Performance Management process that uses three separate performance forms: T1, T2, and T3.

Business Requirement:

  • In the T2 form, display the Overall Rating achieved in the completed T1 form.

  • In the T3 form, display the Overall Ratings achieved in both the completed T1 and T2 forms.

  • The previous ratings should be visible for reference purposes and ideally populate automatically.

Background:
Historically, a single performance form was used. The process has now been redesigned to use separate forms for T1, T2, and T3, creating a requirement to reference ratings from prior forms.

Investigation Performed:

  • We have evaluated PMGM Business Rules but understand that business rules cannot retrieve and populate ratings from previously completed performance forms.

  • We are therefore investigating alternative approaches.

Questions:

  1. Is there any standard SAP SuccessFactors functionality that supports displaying or writing back ratings from one performance form to another?

  2. Can this requirement be achieved using SAP Build Process Automation/Joule?

  3. Can SAP Integration Suite (CPI) be used to extract the overall rating from a completed form and update a field in a subsequent form?

  4. Can Integration Center be used for this scenario, and if so, what objects and fields would be supported?

  5. What is SAP's recommended approach and best practice for maintaining visibility of ratings across multiple performance forms?

Any guidance, limitations, or recommended architecture for this requirement would be appreciated.

entry.title: Message Popover in UI5
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Hope you are doing well.

In this blog, I am going to explain how to create and use a Message Popover in a SAPUI5 application.

What is a Message Popover?

A Message Popover is a control that is used to display errors, warnings, success messages, and informational messages to users in a centralized location.

 

Consider a scenario where a user is signing up and needs to enter details such as their name, phone number, and email address. These fields must be validated to ensure that the entered information is correct.

For example, while entering a name, if the user includes numbers or special characters, the validation should fail. In such cases, an error message can be displayed directly below the input field using the ValueStateText property.

However, when there are multiple validation errors across different fields, displaying messages individually may not provide the best user experience. In such situations, a Message Popover can be used to display all validation messages in one place, making it easier for users to review and resolve them.

Application Overview

To demonstrate the Message Popover functionality, I have created a simple Login application.

The folder structure of the application looks like this:

The application consists of:

  • One View – Contains the login form and footer button.
  • One Fragment – Used to display the Message Popover.

Additionally, I have used three models:

  1. Button Visibility Model – Controls the visibility of the Message Popover button.
  2. Button Type Model – Controls the button type based on the severity of the messages.
  3. mPopover Model – Stores and displays validation messages in the Message Popover.

Using these components, users can view all validation messages in a centralized Message Popover instead of checking each field individually.

Login Form Implementation

I have created a Login form in the view. The form contains two input fields: one for the phone number and another for the password. It also includes a Login button.

Initially, the Login button is hidden. As the user enters the required credentials, validations are performed on both input fields. Once all validation conditions are satisfied and no errors are present, the Login button becomes visible.

In the footer, I have added a Message Popover button. This button is also hidden by default and becomes visible only when there are validation messages to display.

The button displays the total number of messages and dynamically changes its type based on the highest message severity, such as Error, Warning, Success, or Information.

To achieve this functionality, I have used the Button Visibility Model to control the visibility of the Message Popover button and the Button Type Model to manage the button type according to the message severity.

View and Model Configuration

Now, let's look at the View implementation used in this application.

The code below shows the View1 implementation, which contains the login form and the footer button.

<mvc:View xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
    xmlns:form="sap.ui.layout.form"
    xmlns:smartform="sap.ui.comp.smartform"
    xmlns:f="sap.f" controllerName="messagepopover.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc" displayBlock="true"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <content />
        <form:SimpleForm editable="true" class='loginFormClass' id="loginFormClass">
            <Label id="_IDGenLabel1" ></Label>
            <Input placeholder="Enter Your Phone Number" width="300px" liveChange="onChangePhoneNumber" id="loginFragmentPhonNumberInput"></Input>
            <Label id="_IDGenLabel2" ></Label>
            <Input placeholder="Enter Your Password" width="300px" liveChange="onChangePassword" id="loginFragmentPasswordInput"></Input>
            <Label id="_IDGenLabel3" ></Label>
            <Button id="_IDGenButton4" text="Login" type="Emphasized" press="handleLoginButton" visible="{buttonVisibilityModel>/loginButtonInFragment}" width="300px"></Button>
        </form:SimpleForm>
        <footer>
            <OverflowToolbar id="_IDGenOverflowToolbar" >
                <Button id="_IDGenButton5" press="handleMessagePopover" text= "{path: 'mPopOver>/aMockMessage', formatter: '.highestSeverityMessages' }" type="{ path: 'mPopOver>/aMockMessage', formatter: '.buttonTypeFormatter' }"></Button>
            </OverflowToolbar>
        </footer>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

The screenshot below shows the models configured in the manifest.json file. These models are used throughout the application to control button visibility, button type, and the messages displayed in the Message Popover.

The following code snippet shows the data maintained in the Button Visibility Model. This model is responsible for controlling the visibility of the Message Popover button.

{
    "loginButtonInFragment":false
}

The following code snippet shows the data maintained in the Button Type Model. This model is used to dynamically determine the button type based on the severity of the messages.

{
    "messagePopOverButtonType": "Error"
}

The following code snippet shows the data maintained in the mPopover Model. This model stores the messages displayed in the Message Popover, including their type, title, and description.

{
  "aMockMessage": []
}

Phone Number Validation

Now, let's look at the validation logic for the phone number field.

I have attached a liveChange event to the phone number input field so that validation is performed as the user types.

Within this function, I validate multiple scenarios, such as checking whether the field is empty, ensuring that only numeric values are entered, and verifying that the phone number meets the required length.

If any validation condition fails, the corresponding error message is added to the mPopover Model. These messages are then displayed in the Message Popover, allowing users to view all validation errors in a single place.

The code snippet below shows the implementation of the onChangePhoneNumber  function.

onChangePhoneNumber: function (oEvent) {
                debugger
                const phoneNumber = oEvent.getParameter("value")?.trim()||"";
                const buttonVisibilityModel = this.getView().getModel("buttonVisibilityModel");
                const oMessageModel = this.getView().getModel("mPopOver");
                const buttonTypeModel = this.getView().getModel("buttonTypeModel");
                let aMessages = oMessageModel.getProperty("/aMockMessage")
                    .filter(msg => msg.field !== "phone");
                const validations = [
                    {
                        test: v => !v,
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Phone number is required",
                        description: "Please enter your phone number"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => v.length !== 10,
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Phone number must be 10 digits",
                        description: "Enter exactly 10 digits"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => !/^\d+$/.test(v),
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Invalid characters",
                        description: "Phone number must contain only digits"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => !/^[6-9]\d{9}$/.test(v),
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Invalid phone number",
                        description: "Indian phone numbers must start with 6–9"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => /^(\d)\1{9}$/.test(v),
                        type: "Warning",
                        title: "Suspicious phone number",
                        description: "Number seems repetitive, please check again"
                    }
                ];
                const failedRules = validations.filter(rule => rule.test(phoneNumber));
                if (failedRules.length > 0) {
                    failedRules.forEach(rule => {
                        aMessages.push({
                            field: "phone",
                            type: rule.type,
                            title: rule.title,
                            description: rule.description
                        });
                    });
                    const hasError = failedRules.some(rule => rule.type === "Error");
                    oEvent.getSource().setValueState(hasError ? "Error" : "Warning");
                    buttonTypeModel.setProperty(
                        "/message_pop_overButtonType",
                        hasError ? "Error" : "Warning"
                    );
                    phoneNumberFlag = !hasError;
                } else {
                    oEvent.getSource().setValueState("None");
                    oEvent.getSource().setValueStateText("");
                    phoneNumberFlag = true;
                }
                oMessageModel.setProperty("/aMockMessage", aMessages);
                buttonVisibilityModel.setProperty(
                    "/loginButtonInFragment",
                    phoneNumberFlag && passwordFlag
                );
            }

Password Validation

Similarly, I have implemented the onChangePassword function and attached it to the password input field using the liveChange event.

This function validates the password against all required criteria, such as checking whether the field is empty, verifying the minimum password length, and ensuring that the password meets the expected format.

Whenever a validation rule is not satisfied, the corresponding message is added to the mPopover Model. These messages are then displayed in the Message Popover, providing users with clear feedback about the issues that need to be resolved.

The code snippet below shows the implementation of the onChangePassword function.

onChangePassword: function (oEvent) {
                debugger
                const password = oEvent.getParameter("value")?.trim() || "";
                const buttonVisibilityModel = this.getView().getModel("buttonVisibilityModel");
                const oMessageModel = this.getView().getModel("mPopOver");
                const buttonTypeModel = this.getView().getModel("buttonTypeModel");

                let aMessages = oMessageModel.getProperty("/aMockMessage")
                    .filter(msg => msg.field !== "password");

                const validations = [
                    {
                        test: v => !v,
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Password is required",
                        description: "Please enter your password"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => v.length < 8,
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Too short",
                        description: "Password must be at least 8 characters long"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => !/[A-Z]/.test(v),
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Missing uppercase letter",
                        description: "Password must contain at least one uppercase letter"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => !/[a-z]/.test(v),
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Missing lowercase letter",
                        description: "Password must contain at least one lowercase letter"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => !/\d/.test(v),
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Missing number",
                        description: "Password must contain at least one number"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => !/[!@#$%^&*(),.?":{}|<>]/.test(v),
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Missing special character",
                        description: "Password must contain at least one special character"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => /\s/.test(v),
                        type: "Error",
                        title: "Contains whitespace",
                        description: "Password must not contain spaces"
                    },
                    {
                        test: v => /^([a-zA-Z0-9!@#$%^&*])\1{7,}$/.test(v),
                        type: "Warning",
                        title: "Weak password",
                        description: "Password is repetitive and easy to guess"
                    }
                ];
                const failedRules = validations.filter(rule => rule.test(password));
                if (failedRules.length > 0) {
                    failedRules.forEach(rule => {
                        aMessages.push({
                            field: "password",
                            type: rule.type,
                            title: rule.title,
                            description: rule.description
                        });
                    });
                    const hasError = failedRules.some(rule => rule.type === "Error");
                    oEvent.getSource().setValueState(hasError ? "Error" : "Warning");
                    buttonTypeModel.setProperty(
                        "/message_pop_overButtonType",
                        hasError ? "Error" : "Warning"
                    );
                    passwordFlag = !hasError;
                } else {
                    oEvent.getSource().setValueState("None");
                    oEvent.getSource().setValueStateText("");
                    passwordFlag = true;
                }
                oMessageModel.setProperty("/aMockMessage", aMessages);
                buttonVisibilityModel.setProperty(
                    "/loginButtonInFragment",
                    phoneNumberFlag && passwordFlag
                );
            }

Message Popover Fragment

Whenever a validation error occurs, I add the corresponding message to the mPopover Model.

Based on the messages available in the model, the footer button dynamically displays the total number of messages and their severity type.

When the user clicks the footer button, a Message Popover Fragment is opened. This fragment is bound to the mPopover Model and displays all validation messages in a centralized location, making it easier for users to review and resolve issues.

The code snippet below shows the implementation of the Message Popover Fragment.

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    <MessagePopover id="_IDGenMessagePopover" items="{mPopOver>/aMockMessage/}" afterClose="handleMessagePopoverClose">
        <items >
            <MessageItem id="_IDGenMessageItem" type="{mPopOver>type}" title="{mPopOver>title}" activeTitle="{mPopOver>active}" description="{mPopOver>description}" subtitle="{mPopOver>subtitle}" counter="{mPopOver>counter}"></MessageItem>
        </items>
    </MessagePopover>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

Opening the Message Popover

To display the messages stored in the mPopover Model, I have created a function called handleMessagePopover.

This function is triggered when the user clicks the Message Popover button available in the footer.

Inside this function, the Message Popover Fragment is loaded and opened. Since the fragment is bound to the mPopover Model, it automatically displays all validation messages, including errors, warnings, success messages, and informational messages.

By centralizing all messages within the Message Popover, users can easily review and resolve validation issues without having to check each field individually.

The code snippet below shows the implementation of the handleMessagePopover function.

handleMessagePopover: async function (oEvent) {
                debugger
                this.oDialog = await this.loadFragment({
                    name: "messagepopover.fragments.messagePopover",
                }).then(function (oDialog) {
                    return oDialog;
                });
                this.oDialog.openBy(oEvent.getSource());
            }

I have used two formatters in Button in footer one is “highestSeverityMessages” and another one is “buttonTypeFormatter”

Code snippet of “highestSeverityMessages”

highestSeverityMessages: function () {
                debugger
                var sHighestSeverityIconType = this.buttonTypeFormatter();
                var sHighestSeverityMessageType;

                switch (sHighestSeverityIconType) {
                    case "Negative":
                        sHighestSeverityMessageType = "Error";
                        break;
                    case "Critical":
                        sHighestSeverityMessageType = "Warning";
                        break;
                    case "Success":
                        sHighestSeverityMessageType = "Success";
                        break;
                    default:
                        sHighestSeverityMessageType = !sHighestSeverityMessageType ? "Information" : sHighestSeverityMessageType;
                        break;
                }
                return this.getView().getModel('mPopOver').oData.aMockMessage.reduce(function (iNumberOfMessages, oMessageItem) {
                    return oMessageItem.type === sHighestSeverityMessageType ? ++iNumberOfMessages : iNumberOfMessages;
                }, " ");
            }

code snippet of "buttonTypeFormatter" 

buttonTypeFormatter: function () {
                debugger
                var sHighestSeverityIcon;
                var aMessages = this.getView().getModel('mPopOver').getData().aMockMessage;
                if (aMessages) {
                    aMessages.forEach(function (sMessage) {
                        switch (sMessage.type) {
                            case "Error":
                                sHighestSeverityIcon = "Negative";
                                break;
                            case "Warning":
                                sHighestSeverityIcon = sHighestSeverityIcon !== "Negative" ? "Critical" : sHighestSeverityIcon;
                                break;
                            case "Success":
                                sHighestSeverityIcon = sHighestSeverityIcon !== "Negative" && sHighestSeverityIcon !== "Critical" ? "Success" : sHighestSeverityIcon;
                                break;
                            default:
                                sHighestSeverityIcon = !sHighestSeverityIcon ? "Neutral" : sHighestSeverityIcon;
                                break;
                        }
                    });
                    return sHighestSeverityIcon;
                } else {
                    return sHighestSeverityIcon = "Default";
                }
            }

This is how I have implemented MessagePopover

Conclusion

Message Popover is a useful SAPUI5 control for displaying validation messages in a centralized and user-friendly manner. Instead of showing individual error messages across multiple fields, it provides a single location where users can view and manage all messages efficiently.

In this blog, we explored how to implement a Message Popover using a simple Login application. We covered input validation using the liveChange event, storing validation messages in a dedicated model, dynamically controlling the visibility and type of the Message Popover button, and displaying messages through a reusable fragment.

By implementing Message Popover in your applications, you can improve the overall user experience and provide clear, consistent feedback to users during data entry and validation processes.

I hope this blog helps you understand and implement Message Popover in your SAPUI5 applications. Thank you for reading, and feel free to share your feedback or suggestions in the comments section.

entry.title: SAP Architecture Center — центр знань для SAP-архітекторів та розробників
entry.content:
SAP відкрила SAP Architecture Center — публічний хаб офіційних референсних архітектур, best practices та стратегічного бачення від SAP. Це відкритий центр знань, який постійно оновлюється разом із розвитком екосистеми SAP.

Що містить SAP Architecture Center?

Архітектури згруповані за технологічними доменами:

  • AI & Machine Learning
  • Application Development & Automation
  • Data & Analytics
  • Integration
  • Operation & Security

Також варто переглянути ці must-read матеріали:

1.  AI-native North Star Architecture 

Це стратегічний документ SAP про майбутнє enterprise-систем.

Ринок enterprise переживає платформний зсув, не менш значущий, ніж поява інтернету. Agentic AI фундаментально змінює те, як організації функціонують, приймають рішення та конкурують.

Ключова ідея:

Підприємства еволюціонують від систем, що виконують — до систем, що розмірковують, навчаються та адаптуються — до того, що SAP називає Autonomous Enterprise.

Документ описує цільову reference architecture та стратегічне бачення SAP, доступний онлайн та у форматі PDF для завантаження. Оновлюється щоквартально.

2. SAP’s AI Golden Path

Це практичний стартовий гайд для всіх, хто будує AI-рішення на SAP — від вибору технологій до production deployment.

Покриває три архітектурні шари:

  • Agent Layer — Joule Studio (content-based агенти), open-source фреймворки + generative AI hub (code-based агенти), MCP-інструменти
  • AI Layer — SAP AI Core, generative AI hub, SAP HANA Cloud (Vector Engine, Knowledge Graph Engine, PAL, SparkML)
  • Data Layer — SAP HANA Cloud, Business Data Cloud, Databricks, Snowflake

Гайд структурує AI development lifecycle по фазах:

Explore & Discover → Design → Deliver → Run & Scale → Evaluate & Improve → Extend

А також допомагає вибрати правильний AI-підхід:

  • AI Agents ← автоматизація, динамічні рішення
  • GenAI & LLMs ← Текст, діалог, узагальнення
  • Classic ML ← Предикція, класифікація, аномалії, time series
  • Relational Foundation Models ← Структуровані табличні дані без тренування

3. Spotlight

Нещодавно в GitHub-репозиторії з’явився файл AGENTS.md — і це дуже цікава ініціатива.

Уявіть собі README.md, але написаний не для людей, а для AI-агентів. Це новий стандарт від Agentic AI Foundation. Файл надає AI-агенту контекст про проєкт — фреймворки, конвенції, команди — щоб агент одразу "розумів" специфіку репозиторію.

Файл вже підтримується провідними AI-агентами: GitHub Copilot, OpenAI Codex, opencode — вони автоматично завантажують його на початку кожної сесії.

Для кого це?

  • Архітектори — Готові референсні шаблони та north-star орієнтири
  • Розробники на SAP BTP — Гайди, SDK, туторіали, фреймворки
  • Product Managers & AI лідери — Розуміння AI development lifecycle
  • Консультанти та партнери — Спільна мова з SAP та hyperscalers
  • Студенти та всі, хто вивчає SAP — Розуміння сучасної enterprise архітектури

Переходьте на Welcome | SAP Architecture Center та логіньтеся через SAP ID або GitHub.

Радимо розпочати з AI-native North Star Architecture для стратегічного огляду, далі дослідити AI Golden Path для практичних AI-рішень, а якщо ви розробник — склонуйте репозиторій на GitHub та спробуйте AGENTS.md зі своїм AI-агентом.

Архітектура майбутнього вже відкрита для всіх. Час нею скористатися. 🚀

entry.title: Configuring OAuth Credentials for SAP SuccessFactors – A Step by Step Guide
entry.content:
Hello All,

Greetings for the Day!!

Basic Authentication is being phased out for SuccessFactors. If your standard or custom SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) interfaces still rely on Basic Authentication, now is the time to transition to OAuth2.0.

Even though the SAP documentation regarding OAuth2 SAML is comprehensive enough, this article will guide you practically through the setup. It covers the end-to-end configuration across both CPI and SuccessFactors, including generation of certificates and registering your credentials.

This article outlines the exact methodology I utilize during the project implementations. It emphasizes the proper sequence of operations within both systems to ensure you can activate OAuth seamlessly, eliminating the need for trial and error. The steps are straightforward, reproducible, and perfect for distributing among your project members.

Prerequisites

  • Access to your SAP CPI tenant with permissions for Monitor → Keystore and Security Material.
  • Administrator access in SuccessFactors to manage OAuth2 Client Applications
  • SuccessFactors Company ID along with API/Data Center URL
  • A dedicated technical/API user in SuccessFactors

Architecture Overview

From a high level perspective, here is how the OAuth flow functions:

  1. CPI creates a key pair (the certificate);
  2. The certificate is then registered in the SuccessFactors by setting up an OAuth2 client mapped to a technical user;
  3. SuccessFactors generates an API Key specific to this client application.
  4. In CPI, an OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion credential is configured using the client parameters;
  5. Lastly, the iFlow leverages this credential to obtain access tokens, enabling secure communication with SuccessFactors APIs.

Part A - CPI: Create the Key Pair (Steps 1 - 4)

Step 1: Log in to your SAP CPI environment and navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Key store.

Step 2: Click Create → Key Pair.

Step 3: Populate the Key Pair parameters:

  • Alias: Choose a recognizable name for your setup (e.g., successfactorsoAuth).
  • Common Name (CN): Enter the exact SuccessFactors technical user ID.
  • Country/Region: Input your location code, such as IN (India).
  • Validity: set a sufficient validity period (don’t keep default short dates)

Step 4: Hit Create, then choose Download Certificate. You will need this certificate for the SuccessFactors configuration.

Tip: Keep a secure record of both the Alias and the CN, as you will need to reference them later during the CPI OAuth setup.

Part B - Success Factors: Register the OAuth Client (Steps 5 - 9)

Step 5: Sign into Success Factors and use the search tool to find Manage OAuth2 Client Applications.

Step 6: Click Register Client Application.

 

 

 

 

Step 7: Enter application details:

  • Application Name: You can give any meaningful name for your architechture.
  • Application URL: your middleware base URL (up to *.ondemand.com)
  • Check the box for Bind to Users
  • In User IDs section, provide the SF technical user (this must exactly match the CN you used in CPI)

Now, paste the CPI certificate:

  1. Open the downloaded certificate file using a basic text editor (like Notepad).
  2. Copy only the text situated between
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE-----
  3. Paste this string into X.509 Certificate field.

Click Register.

Step 8: Select View next to the newly created application to see its details.

Step 9: Copy the generated API Key and store it safely. This will be required for the CPI configuration.

Common pitfall: If the certificate block includes the BEGIN/END CERTIFICATE lines or extra white space, registration can fail. Only paste the primary block of text.

Part C - CPI: Create OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion Credential (Steps 10 - 12)

Step 10: Return to CPI and navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Security Material.

Step 11: Go to Create → OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.

Step 12: Create a new entry with the following details:

  • Name: An identifiable title (e.g., SF_OAuth_SAML_Prod)
  • Audience: www.successfactors.com
  • Token Service URL: your API host + /oauth/token
    (For instance: https://api4.successfactors.com/oauth/token - make sure use your specific Data Center host).
  • Target System Type: Select SuccessFactors.
  • Company ID: your SF Company ID.
  • User ID: Select Key Pair Common Name (CN).
  • Key Pair Alias: the alias you created (e.g., successfactorsoAuth).
  • Client Key: The API key you retrieved from SuccessFactors.
  • Deploy.

 

Notes: 

  • The User ID must match the user you bound in SuccessFactors.
  • The Key Pair Alias must be exactly the CPI keystore alias.
  • The Token Service URL must be your DC’s API endpoint with /oauth/token.

Part D - Test with a Simple iFlow (Steps 13 - 15)

Step 13: Build a basic test iFlow that calls a simple SuccessFactors API (e.g., a GET on FOCompany or User with minimal fields).

Step 14: In the receiver adaptor settings, apply the following:
Authentication: Set to OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.
Select the credential you created above.

Step 15: Turn HTTP Session Reuse = On Exchange (important).
This minimizes token overhead and prevents intermittent auth failures.

Deploy and execute the test. You should receive a valid response from Success Factors.

Troubleshooting (what usually goes wrong)

401/403 errors:

  • Review your Audience and Token URL (spelling mistakes or incorrect DC hosts happen frequently).
  • Verify that HTTP Session Reuse is turned on in the adapter settings.
  • Double-check the Company ID and User ID in the CPI credential.

Invalid certificate / client errors:

  • Re‑paste only the certificate body exclusively (without BEGIN/END lines).
  • Check the CPI Keystore to make sure the Key Pair hasn't passed its expiration date.

Wrong user context:

  • The User ID applied in “Bind to Users” must be the same user used as CN in the CPI Key Pair and User ID in CPI OAuth credential.

Token URL not reachable:

  • Confirm if the API host is correct for your data center.
  • Ensure there are no proxies/firewalls that might be restricting outgoing connections from CPI to SF.

Best Practices (My Project Recommendations)

  1. Make use of Technical User in SF for API interactions rather than relying on personal or administrator accounts;
  2. Monitor certificate validity – set up calendar reminders 30–45 days before expiry.
  3. Store API Keys securely; refrain from sharing them in casual chats or open documents.
  4. Enable HTTP Session Reuse for the SuccessFactors adapters.
  5. Start with a minimal test iFlow before applying changes to your live business integrations.
  6. Review OAuth clients in SF quarterly; remove unused clients and rotate certs periodically.
  7. Use CPI Monitoring & Traces for root cause; they’re incredibly valuable for OAuth issues.

Conclusion

If you have followed the above mentioned instructions generating the key pair in CPI, OAuth client in SuccessFactors, credential in CPI, and a small test iFlow you’re set for a clean migration from Basic Auth to OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.

The vast majority of errors I encounter are configuration typos or missed details (wrong token URL, incorrect User ID/CN mapping, certificate pasted incorrectly, or HTTP Session Reuse left off). Once those are correct, the flow is very stable.

Implementing this architecture ensures your integrations remain aligned with SAP’s roadmap, preventing you from unexpected disruptions when Basic Authentication is officially retired.

Best Regards,
Rasesh Thakkar.

entry.title: Reducer Pattern for SAPUI5 state management
entry.content:
Introduction

State management in SAPUI5 controllers follow the same pattern: a JSONModel, and some setProperty calls. This works well until you have a lot of fields to manage. As a SAPUI5 developer, I find it really difficult to keep track of all the different setProperty calls, all over the controller.

In this article, I will share how we can adapt the reducer pattern, popularised by Redux, in the SAPUI5 framework for state management. With the reducer pattern, our controller stops managing how state changes and only announces what happened, while the reducer handles the rest. Note that this pattern is most effective for handling complex UI states, and for a simple UI feature, the setProperty calls directly to the model will likely suffice.

---

The problem

Let's use the following scenario as an example. We want to build a "Create User" form and the following three fields are required: username, email, and role. The username must be unique and the provided email must be a of valid format. Fields are bound two-way to the model:

  <Input value="{createUserModel>/username}" change=".onUsernameChange" />
  <Input value="{createUserModel>/email}" change=".onEmailChange" />
  <Select selectedKey="{createUserModel>/role}" change=".onRoleChange" />

In a typical controller it looks like this:

  onUsernameChange(): void {
      const username = this._model.getProperty("/username");
      const email = this._model.getProperty("/email");
      const role = this._model.getProperty("/role");
      this._model.setProperty("/isValidatingUsername", true);
      this._model.setProperty("/isSubmitEnabled", false);

      UserApi.checkUsernameExists(username).then(exists => {
          this._model.setProperty("/isValidatingUsername", false);
          this._model.setProperty("/usernameValueState", exists ? "Error" : "None");
          this._model.setProperty("/usernameValueStateText", exists ? "Username already taken" : "");
          // isSubmitEnabled depends on all three fields — logic duplicated here
          this._model.setProperty("/isSubmitEnabled",
              !!(username && !exists && email && role));
      });
  }

  onEmailChange(): void {
      const username = this._model.getProperty("/username");
      const isValidatingUsername = this._model.getProperty("/isValidatingUsername");
      const role = this._model.getProperty("/role");
      const email = this._model.getProperty("/email");
      const isValid = /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(email);
      this._model.setProperty("/emailValueState", isValid ? "None" : "Error");
      // isSubmitEnabled logic duplicated again
      this._model.setProperty("/isSubmitEnabled",
          !!(username && !isValidatingUsername && email && isValid && role));
  }

Each individual event handler needs to manually check against the model's properties and duplicate the same isSubmitEnabled logic. For every field added to the form, a new event handler is required!

---

The idea

The Reducer Pattern replaces scattered setProperty calls with a single pure function:

(currentState, action) → nextState

The controller dispatches a named action, a plain object describing what happened. The reducer takes the current state and the action and returns the next state, with no side effects.

---

Step 1: Define your state

// types.ts
import type { ValueState } from "sap/ui/core/library";

export interface CreateUserModel {
    username: string;
    email: string;
    role: string;
    usernameValueState: ValueState;
    usernameValueStateText: string;
    isValidatingUsername: boolean;
    emailValueState: ValueState;
    isSubmitEnabled: boolean;
}

---
Step 2: Define your actions

We leverage on the discriminated union concept to ensure type safety of the passed in arguments. Reading this actions file tells you every way the state can change:

// createUserActions.ts
export type CreateUserAction =
    /** Dialog opened — resets everything to initial values. */
    | { type: "DIALOG_OPENED" }
    /** Username field edited — carries async validation state. */
    | { type: "USERNAME_CHANGED"; username: string; usernameError?: string | null; isValidatingUsername?: boolean }
    /** Email field edited — format validated inline. */
    | { type: "EMAIL_CHANGED"; email: string }
    /** Role dropdown changed. */
    | { type: "ROLE_CHANGED"; role: string }

---
Step 3: Write the reducer

All logic as to how the nextState is derived will be found in the reducer file. Derived properties like isSubmitEnabled are computed here:

  // createUserReducer.ts
  import { ValueState } from "sap/ui/core/library";
  import type { CreateUserModel } from "./types";
  import type { CreateUserAction } from "./createUserActions";

  function isEmailValid(email: string): boolean {
      return /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(email);
  }

  function canSubmit(state: CreateUserModel): boolean {
      return !!(
          state.username.trim() &&
          !state.usernameValueStateText &&
          !state.isValidatingUsername &&
          state.email &&
          state.emailValueState === ValueState.None &&
          state.role
      );
  }

  export function createUserReducer(state: CreateUserModel, action: CreateUserAction): CreateUserModel {
      switch (action.type) {
          case "DIALOG_OPENED":
              return {
                  username: "", email: "", role: "",
                  usernameValueState: ValueState.None, usernameValueStateText: "",
                  isValidatingUsername: false, emailValueState: ValueState.None,
                  isSubmitEnabled: false
              };

          case "USERNAME_CHANGED": {
              const usernameError = action.usernameError ?? null;
              const isValidatingUsername = action.isValidatingUsername ?? false;
              const next: CreateUserModel = {
                  ...state,
                  username: action.username,
                  usernameValueState: !action.username.trim() || usernameError ? ValueState.Error : ValueState.None,
                  usernameValueStateText: usernameError ?? "",
                  isValidatingUsername
              };
              return { ...next, isSubmitEnabled: canSubmit(next) };
          }

          case "EMAIL_CHANGED": {
              ...
          }

          case "ROLE_CHANGED": {
              ...
          }

          default:
              return state;
      }
  }

---
Step 4: Wire it into the controller

Firstly, let's add a generic TypedJSONModel type that adds TypeScript types to JSONModel. This allows us to check the path strings at compile time, meaning that  getProperty("/usernaem") becomes a compile error rather than a silent runtime bug:

  // utils/TypedJSONModel.ts
  import JSONModel from "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel";

  export interface TypedJSONModel<T extends object> extends JSONModel {
      getData(): T;
      getProperty<K extends keyof T>(path: `/${string & K}`): T[K];
      setProperty<K extends keyof T>(path: `/${string & K}`, value: T[K]): boolean;
  }

  export function createTypedModel<T extends object>(data: T): TypedJSONModel<T> {
      return new JSONModel(data) as TypedJSONModel<T>;
  }

Then, we add a dispatch method to the controller. It runs the reducer and only calls setProperty for keys that have actually been changed:

  private readonly _model = createTypedModel<CreateUserModel>({ /* initial state */ });

  private dispatch(action: CreateUserAction): void {
      const prev = { ...this._model.getData() };
      const next = createUserReducer(prev, action);
      for (const key of Object.keys(next) as (keyof CreateUserModel)[]) {
          if (prev[key] !== next[key]) {
              this._model.setProperty(`/${key}`, next[key]);
          }
      }
  }

Now, our new event handlers simply reading from the model and dispatch the relevant action!

  onUsernameChange(): void {
      const username = this._model.getProperty("/username");
      this.dispatch({ type: "USERNAME_CHANGED", username, isValidatingUsername: !!username.trim() });
      if (!username.trim()) { return; }

      UserApi.checkUsernameExists(username).then(exists => {
          this.dispatch({
              type: "USERNAME_CHANGED",
              username,
              usernameError: exists ? "Username already taken" : null,
              isValidatingUsername: false
          });
      });
  }

  onEmailChange(): void {
      this.dispatch({ type: "EMAIL_CHANGED", email: this._model.getProperty("/email") });
  }

  onRoleChange(): void {
      this.dispatch({ type: "ROLE_CHANGED", role: this._model.getProperty("/role") });
  }

---

Testing

Since the reducer is a pure function, our unit tests do not require any SAPUI5 framework. The reducer can be tested purely on its own.

  const defaultState: CreateUserModel = {
      username: "", email: "", role: "",
      usernameValueState: ValueState.None, usernameValueStateText: "",
      isValidatingUsername: false, emailValueState: ValueState.None,
      isSubmitEnabled: false
  };

  test("submit disabled when form is empty", () => {
      expect(defaultState.isSubmitEnabled).toBe(false);
  });

  test("submit enabled when all fields are valid", () => {
      let s = createUserReducer(defaultState, { type: "USERNAME_CHANGED", username: "jsmith" });
      s = createUserReducer(s, { type: "EMAIL_CHANGED", email: "jsmith@example.com" });
      s = createUserReducer(s, { type: "ROLE_CHANGED", role: "admin" });
      expect(s.isSubmitEnabled).toBe(true);
  });

---
Conclusion

 Raw setPropertyReducer pattern
Where is state logic?Scattered across handlersOne function, one file
Derived stateRe-derived in every relevant handlerComputed once, inside the reducer
TestabilityNeeds controller + viewPlain function call
Type safetyPath strings fail at runtimeCompile-time path checks via TypedJSONModel
Binding notificationsFire for every setProperty callOnly fire for changed keys

While the setProperty API call is sufficient for simple UI scenarios, it gets very messy if the UI flow is complex. In such complex scenarios, I believe that using the reducer pattern will be extremely helpful. By abstracting out the logic for state/property changes into a reducer, we can keep the controller layer thin and therefore more readable.

Re: DMOVE2S4 – your one-step approach into the cloud

Hi @rajasekhar79! 

Thanks for reaching out!

For conversions to SAP S/4HANA conversions, DMOVE2S4 can be used, and it is not restricted to RISE projects.
Regarding your questions you might want to refer to the official guide.

Software Update Manager will, if started on AAS, offer to relocate the ASCS and "move" the system to target landscape.
Please note that there is no dedicated Primary Application Server anymore (See KBA 2360614). Any other application servers are not considered by Software Update Manager. Please note that there are recommendations regarding network capabilities (see the same page in the guide).

All the best for your project!

Best regards,
Mirja

Re: subcontracting with account assignment Q
entry.content:
Hello @FF2 

Let's see the documentation of the "Subcontracting Supplier" field:

What it does is a goods receipt posting to the special stock with subcontractor. At the same time account assignment Q post to special project stock. You can't post to two different special stocks at the same time.

A very similar situation is explained in the note 2391194 - Movement 541 with Special Stock E or Q - SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: PMGM - Display Overall Rating from Previous Performance Forms
entry.content:
Dear Adele

Hope you're doing well!

There are a few possible workarounds that could help address this requirement:

  1. Performance Form History

    A History icon is available on the Performance Management forms. You may suggest to the customer that users can refer to the form history to view past performance ratings for reference.

  2. Trend Element

    The Overall Performance Rating is captured within the Trend Element. You can create a custom section on the Performance Form and provide a link to the Trend Element. By accessing this link, users will be able to view the Overall Performance Ratings.

    The only caveat with this approach is that the Trend Element will display ratings from all Performance Management forms that have been launched for the employee, rather than only specific review periods.

  3. Custom MDF Object via CPI Integration

    You may explore a custom integration approach using CPI. A custom MDF object can be created, and the Overall Ratings can be populated into this MDF object through CPI. The MDF object can then be exposed on the Performance Form through a custom section or link.

    Alternatively, you can create custom fields such as "T2 Overall Rating" on the T3 Performance Form and have the CPI team extract the rating from the T2 form and populate it into the corresponding custom field on the T3 form.

Hope this might help.

Thanks,

Amaan

entry.title: Re: Required Columns in Standard Report Analyze Stock in...
entry.content:
Good day @cmarajesh_yadav 

Thank you for your question.

For the columns you requested, Total Receipt Value and Total Issue Value are not fields that you can normally add to the SAP delivered app by simply configuring them if they are not already available in the app’s column settings.

First, check Analyse Stock in Date Range by opening the app, running the report, and checking Settings à Columns. If the requested receipt and issue value fields are not available there, you cannot add them as standard columns through SSCUI or app personalisation. The app is a standard analytical app for monitoring goods movement postings and displaying stock quantities and values in a defined date range.

For your current business need, either build a custom analytical report that classifies movements into receipt and issue values, or submit an improvement request asking SAP to add the two calculated columns to the standard app.

See the following reference for more insights.

Bestand im Datumsbereich analysieren | SAP Help Portal

3649511 - No option to add Total on Value and Quantity in the APP 'Material Documents Overview' - SAP for Me

I recommend the following:

  1. Check whether the required fields exist under Settings --> Columns in Analyse Stock in Date Range.                                                                           
  2. If they are not available, document the gap with screenshots.
  3. Use Custom CDS Views and Custom Analytical Queries to build a custom report for receipt value and issue value.                                                                                                 
  4. Classify the value logic by movement type, stock change direction, amount, currency, and reversal logic.
  5. Reconcile the custom report with Analyze Stock in Date Range.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Exporting validation rule by rule status
entry.content: Hi colleagues, in your current version. the validation method is used as a group for all the rules that should be validated. so you can download from validation method and mark the 'include reference objects' to download all included rules in the validation method. With the new design for balance validation since version OP2025, manage balance validation rules and groups application will replace the validation method design. But from the business concept itself, it remains unchanged.

entry.title: Integration Suite sharing within company group - how to organize content
entry.content:
Hi all,

I'm working for a company which is part of a group. Until now, each company of the group uses several CPIs on NEO. We will need to migrate to Integration Suite now. For costing reasons, there is one global account owned by the group and shared with all companies. The Integration Suite landscape will consist of two subaccounts (test and prod). These subaccounts will be shared with all companies of the group due to costing reasons.

As same IS subaccounts will be used by various companies, administration and development as well as monitoring will be handled by different people at different locations and working for different companies. If we do this in an uncontrolled manner, it will become a mess. 

We need to find a way on how to organize all the content, artifacts, partner details etc. in a meaningful manner. For this, I want to define some governance of how to organize artifacts, naming conventions, authorization rules etc.

As I'm quite new to IS, I'm struggling with defining such a governance. I found lots of learning content but most of them are somewhat of fundamental. Create one project, create first flow and that's it.
What I've learned so far is that there is no hierarchy within integration projects, like we do have in ABAP packages. As projects are the most fundamental part of organizing content, I wonder if we should organize it by company, integration scenario (CPI for C4C, C4s, S/4, non-SAP), business area (Sales, Production) or in a different way?

I didn't found any best practices and for this, I want to ask you to point me into the right direction. How do you handle complex integration landscapes? Do you have some link to a good resource to best practices?

entry.title: Re: How to Restrict Users from Accessing Customer Invoice...
entry.content:
Hello,

It is currently possible to restrict user access to Customer Invoices and Supplier Invoices only at the Company level. At present, there is no option to restrict access based on Business Residence or Site level.

This is the expected and standard system behavior.
For more information, please refer to SAP Note KBA 3426144, "How To Set Access Restriction Rights for User on Org Unit Level?" This KBA also illustrates the current system behavior regarding access restrictions.

If you require this functionality, it would be considered a new enhancement request. Please submit your idea through the SAP Influence Portal:
# https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/886 


Kind regards,
Pavithra

entry.title: SAF‑T (PT) Payments/Clearings: Why scenarios with more than one Business Partner are not allowed
entry.content:
This blog explains why certain payment scenarios cannot be represented compliantly in SAF‑T (PT) 1.04_01, how this impacts SAP customers today, and what the SAF‑T XSD schema explicitly enforces when it comes to Payments, Customers, and Suppliers. 

Problem Scope 

The issue under discussion affects a very specific but impactful set of scenarios related with incoming manual payments and clearing transactions. 

Typical business cases:  

  • A single payment that settles open items for multiple customers 
  • Clearing transactions between customer and vendor accounts 

 

 

Technical Deep Dive: What the SAF‑T XSD Actually Allows 

To understand why this happens, we need to look directly at the SAF‑T (PT) 1.04_01 XSD schema, not at business expectations. 

 

 

Can a Payment Contain More Than One Customer?No - This is explicitly forbidden by the schema

The XSD defines a key reference constraint that binds each <Payment> to exactly one <CustomerID>: 

XML 

<xs:keyref name="PaymentPaymentRefNoCustomerIDConstraint" 

refer="CustomerIDConstraint"> 

<xs:selector xpath="ns:SourceDocuments/ns:Payments/ns:Payment" /> 

<xs:field xpath="ns:CustomerID" /> 

</xs:keyref> 

 A single SAF‑T Payment cannot represent a settlement covering multiple customers, even if the payment exists as one document in SAP. 

 

 

Can a Payment Reference a Supplier? No - Suppliers are not permitted in the Payment structure

Evidence from the Schema 

The Payment section includes the following constraints: 

  • PaymentPaymentRefNoConstraint 
  • PaymentPaymentRefNoCustomerIDConstraint 

There is no: 

  • SupplierID 
  • SupplierIDConstraint 
  • Alternative party element 

This design clearly indicates that: 

  • Payments are customer‑only constructs 
  • Supplier involvement is not supported, neither instead nor in addition to customers 

A SAF‑T Payment cannot reference suppliers, and any clearing between customer and vendor accounts inherently violates the schema. 

 

Closing Thoughts 

Understanding what the SAF‑T schema allows—not what we wish it allowed—is essential for designing compliant solutions. While SAP can improve validations and guidance, certain business scenarios will always require process alignment or redesign to remain SAF‑T compliant. 

Until preventive checks are introduced, customers should be actively discouraged from using multi‑party payment and clearing scenarios when SAF‑T reporting is required. 

 

Cheers, Elsa

entry.title: Project Budget Inquiry – Grouping by Project Type in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I have a question regarding Project Budget inquiries in Forge/ANA Public Cloud, and I haven’t been able to find a solution.

We use the Project Type field to categorize our projects. This field is configured as a selectable list, allowing users to assign a specific project type to each project.

The issue comes when analyzing project budgets. What I need is a report or inquiry that shows all projects with their Committed Costs and Actual Costs, grouped by Project Type.

In the budget inquiry application, I can use Project Type as a filter at the top of the screen, but I cannot group the results by this field in the output. As a result, if I have 10 different project types, I would need to run the same inquiry 10 times and manually consolidate the information.

Is there any way to:

Group budget inquiry results by Project Type?
Include Project Class as a grouping field in reports or inquiries?
Create a faster or more efficient solution to analyze committed and actual costs by project type?

Also, if the standard Project Class field cannot be used for grouping or reporting purposes, I would be open to creating a custom Z field and using that instead

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Portugal – Support for Original / Duplicate / Triplicate Identification in Billing Document Output
entry.content:
Dear SAP,

We would like to request clarification regarding the legal document output requirements in Portugal.

According to our customer's business and compliance requirements, billing documents (Invoices, Credit Notes and similar fiscal documents) must be printable in multiple copies identified as:

  • Original
  • Duplicate
  • Triplicate

Additionally, when a document is reprinted, it should be possible to identify the corresponding copy version accordingly.

At the moment, we have not identified a standard functionality in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that allows the automatic identification of these document copies within the output form.

Could you please clarify:

  • Whether this functionality is currently supported in standard;
  • Whether there are any recommended approaches to achieve this requirement;
  • Whether this requirement is already known by the Localization or Output Management teams.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,

entry.title: Re: Incorrect Planned Order Quantity Calculation in MD11...
entry.content:
Why this happens is because Scrap percentage (MRP1 view) is designed to ensure sufficient net requirement coverage. When you run MRP, the system calculates backward from the requirement, factoring in scrap, then applies lot-sizing rules. In MD11, however, you are entering the order quantity directly, and SAP adds scrap on top of it rather than recalculating against the fixed lot size.

If you want MD11 to behave like MRP, you need either enhancement / User Exit to adjust quantity calculation during planned order creation (e.g., MD_PLDORD_POST) or Process alignment and encourage users to create planned orders via MRP runs rather than manually, if consistency is critical.

entry.title: Re: Can AI Agent replace IT Architects (EA/SA)?
entry.content:
Actually, the irony is that while SAP keeps debating “whether AI agents can replace architects,” the top 5% of the industry has already moved on. It was the same back in the 90s, when we were debating if our cloud CRM iDECS would replace Excel.

Agentic AI with deep architecture skill‑packs — governance, modeling, integration, security, regression mapping — is already automating 92% of ERP customization work in firms like Accenture, PwC, NextERP, Odoo, and others who operate without the legacy bloat.

Claude Mythos is collapsing multi‑year SAP/ERP development cycles into hours, generating full blueprints, extensions, tests, and impact analyses before a human even touches the spec. Architects aren’t being replaced — the manual SAP-era sludge around them IS.

This isn’t hype. It’s just that the frontier is moving faster than the siloed, over‑engineered, data‑bloated ecosystems that still think “AI is coming someday.”

AI isn’t coming for architects. It’s coming for the fat bloat and unstructured siloed data, that made architecture slow.

entry.title: Source storage bin XX cannot be found in warehouse XX in Record defect app
entry.content:
Faced this issue in record defect app of S4 system. 

Here I was expecting the storage bin in Decentralized ewm will be dispalyed in F4. But its not the case. It can only be manually entered. Also was getting this error message, even the storage bin is available in decentralized EWM - In Warehouse Defect with Decentralized EWM | SAP Help Portal its mentioned that, if the user dont have the access then system will throw the error. But for my user id there is no access issue.
Later got to know that the S4 will use RFC user in decentralized EWM to validate the storage bin in EWM from S4. So the user maintained in SM59 for the EWM system to be tested for access issue SU53. 

 

RFC_TYPE FUGR RFC_NAME QFEWM
RFC_TYPE FUNC RFC_NAME QFEWM_GET_STORAGEBIN_INFO

Once maintained the access to that user in EWM the validation works fine in record defect app.

entry.title: SAP BTP vs. Power Platform
entry.content:
Two Languages, One Architecture — Pointing to Industry 6.0

Everyone frames them as competitors. They're not!
Microsoft Power Platform and SAP BTP are two dialects of the same architectural philosophy:
→ Push intelligence to the edge.
→ Dissolve data silos.
→ Turn operational noise into real-time decisions.
→ Let the people closest to the problem build the solution.
The mapping is almost symmetrical:
Power Apps ↔ SAP Build Apps
Power Automate ↔ SAP Build Process Automation
Dataverse ↔ HANA Cloud
Power BI ↔ SAP Analytics Cloud
Azure IoT ↔ SAP Digital Manufacturing + Edge Services
Different SDKs. Same conviction: The factory floor, the supply chain, and the design studio deserve the same composability that software engineering has had for 20 years.

Industry 5.0 — Skipped
Industry 4.0 gave us connectivity. Industry 5.0 demands intentionality:
- Human-in-the-loop creativity
- Regenerative supply chains
- Systems that adapt to people
- Technology that amplifies craft

Most enterprise platforms optimise processes. Very few optimise purpose.
They automate workflows — but not wisdom. They scale operations — but not human imagination. This is the GAP.

So We Built the Missing Layer.
After 20+ years of integrating cloud ecosystems, stitching platforms together, and watching the same fragmentation repeat itself, we arrived at a simple conclusion:
No existing system could unify creativity → supply chain → manufacturing → marketing → sales → support into one living, self-evolving operating layer.

So we built AuraOS, ready for Industry 6.0
Not a tool. Not an app. A semantic operating system — where data, agents, workflows, and human creativity run as one continuous organism.

AuraOS in Action: Sketch → Fabrication in One Agentic Flow
Today, AuraOS deploys the Sketch2Fabrication pipeline:
Signal → Idea → Concept → Material → Machine → Artifact → GTM
From a hand-drawn sketch to a market-listed product — orchestrated by:
- Semantic understanding across every stage
- Multi-agent collaboration with human-in-the-loop checkpoints
- Sustainable material logic is baked into the decision tree
- Closed-loop feedback from production to design
- A regenerative, circular manufacturing mindset
This is Agentic AI, not as a feature. It's an operating philosophy.

So What?
The organizations that will define Industry 6.0 won't be the ones choosing Power Platform or SAP BTP.
They'll be the ones building cohesive, edge-to-cloud ecosystems — where data, automation, creativity, and intelligence flow without friction.

That's the work I love: Designing architectures that unify operations, elevate teams, and turn complexity into clarity. Because in the end, platforms evolve. Architecture endures.

What's your experience bridging MPP/SAP in manufacturing?

##IndustryArchitecture #SAPBTP #AgenticAI
#EdgeToCloud #DigitalManufacturing #SmartFactory

entry.title: Clarification on Outbox Behavior in Multi-Instance Deployments
entry.content:
Questions:

  1. Worker thread distribution: Are worker threads created per application instance, or is there a global pool shared across all instances?

  2. Multi-instance coordination: How do workers across multiple application instances coordinate to avoid processing the same outbox message? 

  3. Ordered outbox guarantees: For ordered outboxes, how is sequential processing per tenant enforced when workers span multiple application instances?

  4. Resource planning: With thousands of tenants in a multi-instance deployment, what's the recommended approach to sizing worker pools to balance throughput and resource consumption?

  Context:

  We're running a multi-tenant application with horizontal scaling and need to understand the threading model and coordination mechanisms to properly plan capacity and ensure correct outbox processing behavior.

Thanks,

-Vinay.
